---
title: "Jumbo Squid Stock Status: How the Chile, China & CALAMASUR Models Differ"
subtitle: "SC13 — SPRFMO Scientific Committee, comparative note"
#author: "Prepared for review"
date: "2026-07-12"
# format:
#   html:
#     toc: true
#     toc-depth: 3
#     number-sections: true
---

## Why the three assessments disagree

All three groups assessed the same species (*Dosidicus gigas*), the same region
(Southeast Pacific / FAO Area 87), and broadly the same catch data updated to 2024
— including the sharp ~52% drop in landings in 2024. Yet they reached different
verdicts on stock status. [The divergence comes almost entirely from **model
architecture** and **how each model treats the 2024 catch collapse and
environmental variability**, not from the underlying data]{.highlight}. The
sections below walk through where the models agree and where they part ways.

## The three modelling approaches at a glance

| Dimension | **SPiCT** [@paya2025spict] | **Bayesian state-space** [@li2025china] | **Depletion + Pella-Tomlinson** [@roaureta2025calamasur] |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model family | Surplus production (Pella-Tomlinson) in **continuous time**, state-space | Surplus production (Pella-Tomlinson) in **discrete (annual) time**, state-space | **Two-stage**: monthly generalized *depletion* model → Pella-Tomlinson surplus production |
| Estimation | Maximum likelihood (TMB) | Bayesian MCMC (WinBUGS) | Non-Bayesian hierarchical (ADMB) |
| Time step | Continuous (month/quarter capable) | Annual | Stage 1 monthly; Stage 2 annual |
| Data used | Catch + 6 country CPUE indices (or 1 global) | Catch + 4 fleet indices (Peru, Chile, Asia) | Monthly catch, **effort** & mean weight (3 fleets) + FAO annual landings |
| Uses fishing **effort**? | No (index-based) | No (index-based) | **Yes** — depletion model is effort-driven, not CPUE-driven |
| Environment in model | Not explicit (discussed as caveat) | **Explicit** — K, r, s vary by ENSO phase; 7 models tested | **Explicit** — r (and tested K, s) vary by El Niño/La Niña; 16 hypotheses |
| Time span | 2001–2024 (and 2012–2024) | 2012–2024 | 2012–2024 (Stage 1); 1990–2024 (Stage 2) |
| 2025/terminal verdict | Kobe **red zone** (best case) | **Healthy** since 2013 (70–88% prob.) | **Not overfished**, possibly overfishing |

## Where they fundamentally differ

### 1. Depletion model vs. pure production model

The single biggest structural difference is CALAMASUR's **two-stage depletion
approach**. Chile (SPiCT) and China both fit a *surplus production model directly
to catch and a CPUE abundance index* — they infer biomass from the relationship
between catch and a relative abundance signal. CALAMASUR instead first fits a
**generalized depletion model** at monthly steps that uses **fishing effort** (not
CPUE) as the mechanistic driver, tracking within-season depletion and recruitment
pulses for each fleet; the biomass series it produces then *feeds* a Pella-Tomlinson
model at Stage 2.

This matters because production models assume the CPUE index is proportional (or a
known function) of true abundance — an assumption widely questioned for fast-growing,
highly migratory squid. CALAMASUR's design deliberately avoids relying on
standardized CPUE as an abundance index, estimating natural mortality *inside* the
model instead (≈2.0/yr, consistent with the short life cycle).

### 2. How the environment enters

China and CALAMASUR both build the ENSO cycle **into** the model, letting productivity
parameters switch between El Niño and La Niña regimes — China favoured a model where
both carrying capacity *K* and growth rate *r* vary (the "Kr" model), while CALAMASUR
found best support for **only *r*** varying (higher in El Niño: 2.93/yr vs 0.48/yr in
La Niña). Chile's SPiCT run does **not** encode the environment explicitly; the
authors instead flag environmental variability and the three squid phenotypes as
caveats to be addressed in future work.

### 3. What each does with the 2024 catch drop

This is the crux of the differing verdicts:

- **China** interprets the healthy stock *despite* the 2024 landings collapse as
  evidence of an El Niño effect on population dynamics — the drop reflects
  environment/availability, not depletion. Verdict: still healthy.
- **CALAMASUR** sees a stock in a **regime of wide fluctuations** with no sustained
  downward biomass trend and exploitation mostly below 40%; not overfished, but the
  rising exploitation rate means overfishing *may* be occurring, and MSY/B~MSY~ are
  judged inadequate reference points.
- **Chile** reads the 52% catch reduction plus a **general decline in the abundance
  indices** as a genuine downturn, placing the 2025 stock in the Kobe **red zone**
  (in the best-fitting, six-index case). SPiCT trusts the declining CPUE signal more
  directly.

### 4. Reference points and management outputs

Chile's SPiCT produces conventional Kobe-plot status (B/B~MSY~, F/F~MSY~) and explicit
**2026 TAC advice** (267–989 kt across cases; ~467 kt in the best case). China produces
Kobe status with terminal-year **healthy-status probabilities** (58–88%). CALAMASUR
argues that under strong fluctuations **MSY and B~MSY~ are not valid reference points**
and a standard Kobe plot cannot be built, calling instead for new environment-aware
reference points based on mean latent productivity (still imprecise).

## Points of genuine agreement

Despite the different headlines, the three assessments converge on several things:
the stock is heavily influenced by the **ENSO cycle**; **biomass fluctuates widely**
rather than showing a clean trend; **exploitation has been rising** in recent years;
and the **standardized CPUE indices need improvement** (spatiotemporal structure,
multiple phenotypes/sizes). Both China and CALAMASUR independently conclude that
environmental cycles drive productivity, and even Chile — while not modelling it —
names the three phenotypes and environmental variability as the key unresolved issues.

## How to read the disagreement

The spread of verdicts is best understood as a **model-structure spectrum** rather
than a data dispute:

- The more a model **trusts declining CPUE as a direct abundance signal** (Chile,
  SPiCT), the more pessimistic the status.
- The more a model **attributes the 2024 drop to environment/availability** (China),
  the more optimistic the status.
- The more a model **relies on effort-based depletion dynamics and treats MSY as
  unreliable under fluctuations** (CALAMASUR), the more it lands on a cautious middle
  — "not overfished, watch the exploitation rate."

No single model is definitively "right"; each encodes a different, defensible
hypothesis about *why* catches fell in 2024. That is precisely why the SPRFMO Squid
Working Group runs them in parallel.

## Sources

- SC13-SQ06-CHL-Update-of-the-SPiCT-applied-to-Dosidicus-gigasin-FAO-area-87.pdf
- SC13-SQ07-CHN-Assessment-of-jumbo-squid-in-Southeast-Pacific-Ocean-based-on-state-space-biomass-dynamics-model-and-impacts-of-ENSO-.pdf
- SC13-Obs03-CALAMASUR-Stock-assessment-of-jumbo-flying-squid-.pdf
